SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claim—that Grid Beam Search enforces lexical constraints during decoding without retraining—and provides experimental evidence across multiple datasets (including simulated interactive editing with >20 BLEU gains) and comparisons to standard beam search. The algorithmic description and complexity (O(ktc)) are given and practical limitations (runtime, unseen tokens) are acknowledged, but important reproducibility details are missing (code not mentioned) and there is limited reporting on statistical significance and wall‑clock/runtime tradeoffs. Overall the technical approach appears sound and experimentally supported, with only minor gaps in evaluation rigor and deployment analysis. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments demonstrating large BLEU gains (including >20 BLEU in simulated interactive editing) and by a concrete algorithmic description of Grid Beam Search that handles multi-token and discontinuous constraints. The paper acknowledges and analyzes practical limitations (O(ktc) runtime increase, need for subword units for unseen tokens) and reports hyperparameters and sensible baselines (standard time-based beam search and NMT baseline). There is no indication of formal mathematical proofs beyond the algorithmic construction, and the summary did not mention statistical significance testing, so minor methodological rigor issues remain, but these do not undermine the core technical correctness and empirical support.

Overall the technical approach appears sound and experimentally supported, with only minor gaps in evaluation rigor and deployment analysis.

There is no indication of formal mathematical proofs beyond the algorithmic construction, and the summary did not mention statistical significance testing, so minor methodological rigor issues remain, but these do not undermine the core technical correctness and empirical support.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles a practical and important problem—incorporating lexical constraints into neural sequence generation without retraining—which is highly relevant for post-editing and domain adaptation workflows. The proposed Grid Beam Search supports multi-token and discontinuous constraints and reportedly yields large BLEU gains (e.g., >20 points in simulated interactive editing), suggesting strong practical utility that could change practitioner tooling. While the naive O(ktc) runtime and lack of implementation details may limit immediate adoption, the technique is general and likely to be widely built upon and to open follow-up work on efficiency and integration into production systems. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—injecting lexical constraints into neural sequence generation without retraining—which is highly relevant for post-editing, interactive translation and domain adaptation. By supporting multi-token and discontinuous constraints and demonstrating large BLEU gains (e.g., >20 points in simulated interactive editing), GBS provides a pragmatic tool that will change how practitioners integrate user constraints into production systems. The approach is broadly applicable (no model changes) and likely to spawn follow-up work on efficiency and richer constraint types, though runtime overhead and reliance on subword handling temper its transformative potential.

While the naive O(ktc) runtime and lack of implementation details may limit immediate adoption, the technique is general and likely to be widely built upon and to open follow-up work on efficiency and integration into production systems.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides substantial experimental evidence across multiple corpora (Autodesk post‑editing, Europarl, CommonCrawl, JRC‑Aquis, OpenSubtitles) and reports large BLEU gains in targeted scenarios (e.g., simulated interactive editing), supporting the central claims. Related work appears adequately covered and practical limitations (naive O(ktc) runtime, unseen token risk mitigated by subword units) are acknowledged. However, the summary does not indicate systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of beam size, number/length of constraints) nor availability of code, which weakens reproducibility and understanding of robustness. Overall the experiments are solid but leave some important analyses and release details unexamined. | B(4): The paper provides solid empirical evidence across multiple datasets (Autodesk post-editing, Europarl, CommonCrawl, JRC-Aquis, OpenSubtitles) and shows large BLEU gains and practical use-cases (interactive post-editing, domain adaptation), supporting the central claims. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the authors discuss key limitations (runtime O(ktc), handling unseen tokens via subword units) and report hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility; however code availability is not mentioned. Missing are more systematic ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of beam size, constraint complexity, or alternative decoding baselines) and runtime/quality trade-off studies, so while thorough, the experimental analysis has some notable gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides substantial experimental evidence across multiple corpora (Autodesk post‑editing, Europarl, CommonCrawl, JRC‑Aquis, OpenSubtitles) and reports large BLEU gains in targeted scenarios (e.g., simulated interactive editing), supporting the central claims. Related work appears adequately covered and practical limitations (naive O(ktc) runtime, unseen token risk mitigated by subword units) are acknowledged. However, the summary does not indicate systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of beam size, number/length of constraints) nor availability of code, which weakens reproducibility and understanding of robustness. Overall the experiments are solid but leave some important analyses and release details unexamined.

Missing are more systematic ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of beam size, constraint complexity, or alternative decoding baselines) and runtime/quality trade-off studies, so while thorough, the experimental analysis has some notable gaps.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and states its main contributions early (GBS for enforcing lexical constraints) with a clear method section that explains the grid-beam structure and runtime complexity (O(ktc)). Notation appears consistent and the experimental setup (datasets, baselines, BLEU metric, hyperparameters) is reported, while figures/tables and examples effectively illustrate the method and results. Writing quality is generally good and readable, though practical details about wall-clock performance, parallelization trade-offs, and availability of code could be more explicitly presented to aid reproducibility. | B(4): The paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from introduction through method (GBS) to experiments and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and framed around practical use cases (interactive post-editing, domain adaptation). Notation and method description appear consistent and the use of subword handling and runtime complexity are clearly explained, while figures/tables/examples (e.g., multi-token/discontinuous constraint examples) effectively illustrate the approach. Minor presentation issues remain—e.g., runtime trade-offs and some implementation details could be more tightly summarized—but overall the writing is good and accessible to researchers and practitioners.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NMT/sequence-decoding problem (lexically constrained decoding) with a practical algorithm (Grid Beam Search) and empirical evaluation on standard MT corpora (Europarl, CommonCrawl, JRC-Aquis, OpenSubtitles, Autodesk post-editing) using BLEU, matching expectations for a top-tier NLP/ML venue. The work targets the right audience (NMT researchers and practitioners), uses appropriate methodology and baselines (standard beam search, clear hyperparameter reporting), and demonstrates real-world applications (interactive post-editing, domain adaptation) that are highly relevant to the community. The stated limitations (runtime complexity, subword handling) are candid and actionable, and the problem formulation and evaluation standards align with venue norms. | B(5): This paper addresses a core problem in neural sequence generation (lexically constrained decoding) with a practical algorithm (Grid Beam Search) and evaluates on standard MT corpora and post-editing scenarios, making it highly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. The method supports multi-token and discontinuous constraints, demonstrates large BLEU gains in interactive settings, and uses expected baselines and metrics, which suits the community’s interests in constrained generation and usable translation tools. While it notes runtime trade-offs, the practical focus (post-editing, domain adaptation) and clear empirical benefits make it an excellent fit for a top conference.

The work targets the right audience (NMT researchers and practitioners), uses appropriate methodology and baselines (standard beam search, clear hyperparameter reporting), and demonstrates real-world applications (interactive post-editing, domain adaptation) that are highly relevant to the community. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): From a methodological perspective the paper presents a notable and concrete extension to standard beam search — the Grid Beam Search (GBS) formulation, coverage vectors and open/closed hypothesis bookkeeping — that meaningfully broadens what can be enforced at decode time (multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints) without retraining. The paper also analyzes algorithmic behavior (naive O(ktc) complexity and per-column parallelism) and explicitly separates its mechanisms from standard time-based beam search, which would inform practitioners designing constrained decoders. Given the provided context (no earlier distinct prior work surfaced other than duplicate entries of this same paper), the contribution is more than incremental but not a radical paradigm shift, so I rate it as notable novelty (4/5). | B(4): The paper proposes Grid Beam Search (GBS), a concrete decoding algorithm that explicitly handles multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints and enforces coverage vectors to prevent repetition—features not present in standard beam search baselines and not shown in the retrieved prior work (only duplicates of this paper were found). The formulation (open/closed hypotheses, constraint coverage) and practical considerations (parallelizable beams, complexity O(ktc)) constitute a non-trivial extension of existing constrained decoding approaches and are clearly differentiated in the summary. While the idea of constrained decoding has been explored, the specific grid/coverage mechanism and support for discontinuous constraints would be novel and informative to experts, though it reads as a notable rather than revolutionary advance in the space.

The paper also analyzes algorithmic behavior (naive O(ktc) complexity and per-column parallelism) and explicitly separates its mechanisms from standard time-based beam search, which would inform practitioners designing constrained decoders. MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares GBS against a standard NMT baseline and time-based beam search on the same datasets using BLEU, and reports detailed runtime complexity (O(ktc)) and substantial BLEU gains (e.g., >20 BLEU in simulated interactive editing), so comparisons are largely fair. However, it appears to omit comparisons to other specialized constrained decoding methods or alternative practical heuristics beyond standard beam search, which is a notable omission given the paper’s focus; related work is described as adequate but no competing implementations or prior constrained-decode baselines are empirically evaluated. Overall the experimental setup and metrics are appropriate and fairly applied, but the set of baselines is not comprehensive.  | B(3): The paper compares GBS against a solid baseline (standard time-based beam search) using the same datasets and BLEU metric, so experimental conditions appear fair. However, comparisons are limited to the NMT baseline and do not include other existing constrained-decoding or constraint-injection techniques (multi-token / discontinuous constraint methods) that would be natural competitors, nor is there evidence the paper acknowledges when prior methods match or exceed its performance. While related-work coverage is described as adequate, the empirical baselines are incomplete for convincingly demonstrating GBS’s advantages in the broader constrained-decoding literature.

main_claims: Grid Beam Search (GBS) extends beam search to allow inclusion of pre-specified lexical constraints (words or phrases) into sequence generation without modifying model parameters or training data.
GBS supports multi-token and discontinuous lexical constraints and can place constraints correctly while having global positive effects on translation quality.
GBS can be applied to interactive post-editing and domain adaptation scenarios and yields large improvements in translation quality (e.g., >20 BLEU points in simulated interactive editing).
_summary.methodology: Introduce Grid Beam Search (GBS), which organizes beams across timesteps and constraint coverage to enforce lexical constraints (including multi-token and discontinuous constraints) during decoding; it uses subword units to handle unknown tokens and can be parallelized across beams.
limitations: Naive GBS runtime complexity is O(ktc), i.e., increased computational cost compared to standard beam search.
Risk that user-specified constraints contain tokens unseen in the model's training data (mitigated by using subword representations).
Practical speed depends on keeping beam size small; otherwise computing hypothesis states dominates time.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - neural machine translation / constrained sequence decoding

reviewer_a.name: Methodology Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating production neural sequence models, including deployment of NMT systems and constrained decoding components.
traits: carefully checks experimental design: baselines, ablations, hyperparameter settings, and statistical significance
looks for reproducibility: clear algorithmic descriptions, pseudo-code, runtime/memory analysis, and available implementation/data
evaluates robustness and scalability: behavior on long sequences, many constraints, and wall-clock performance/parallelism
expects error analysis and failure cases (when constraints hurt output) and comparisons to simple heuristics
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on whether the experiments and implementation details support the claims and whether the method can be reliably reproduced and deployed.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic MT Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Professor and researcher in machine translation and constrained decoding, with a track record of work on lexically constrained generation, domain adaptation, and interactive translation interfaces.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior work in constrained decoding and sequence generation
values practical impact: applicability to real-world workflows (post-editing, domain adaptation) and clarity about limitations
checks theoretical clarity and breadth: whether the approach covers multi-token, discontinuous constraints and how it generalizes
looks for insights about trade-offs (quality vs. fluency vs. constraint satisfaction) and guidance for practitioners
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I emphasize how the contribution advances the field, its relation to existing literature, and the practical significance and limitations of the proposed approach.
